name: Pink Triangle
description: Originally a downward-pointing pink triangle sewn onto the uniforms of gay men in Nazi concentration camps. Reclaimed by the LGBTQ+ community in the 1970s as a symbol of remembrance and resistance, most notably by ACT UP during the AIDS crisis.
current_use: Memorial, activism, and pride symbol. Often worn to honor those persecuted under Nazi rule and during the AIDS epidemic.

name: Lambda
description: Adopted by the Gay Activists Alliance in 1970 and later by the International Gay Rights Congress in 1974. The lambda (Greek letter L) has several claimed meanings: liberation, energy, unity, or a 'complete exchange of energy' in physics.
current_use: Gay rights symbol; used in organization names worldwide (Lambda Legal).

name: Rainbow Infinity
description: An infinity symbol rendered in rainbow colors, originating in the autism self-advocacy community to represent neurodiversity. Now broadly used by the wider neurodivergent community including autistic, ADHD, and dyslexic individuals.
current_use: Neurodiversity symbol; often appears in LGBTQ+ spaces given the significant overlap between LGBTQ+ and neurodivergent communities.

name: Double Venus
description: Two interlocking Venus (female) symbols, representing lesbian identity. Emerged from feminist and lesbian liberation movements in the early 1970s.
current_use: Lesbian pride and identity symbol.

name: Double Mars
description: Two interlocking Mars (male) symbols, representing gay male identity. Adopted as part of the wave of LGBTQ+ symbolic language that developed in the 1970s.
current_use: Gay male pride and identity symbol.

name: Mars-Venus Interlock
description: An interlocking Mars and Venus symbol representing bisexuality. One of several symbols used to represent attraction to multiple genders.
current_use: Bisexual identity symbol, often paired with the bisexual pride flag colors.

name: Transgender Symbol
description: A combination of the Mars symbol, Venus symbol, and a merged Mars/Venus symbol representing a third gender. Designed to represent transgender and intersex people. The ⚧ Unicode character was added in Unicode 4.1 (2005).
current_use: Transgender, intersex, and nonbinary identity symbol. Widely used in activism and as emoji (⚧️).

name: Black Triangle
description: Originally used in Nazi concentration camps to mark women deemed 'asocial', a category that included lesbians. Reclaimed by the lesbian community as a pride and remembrance symbol in the 1970s–1990s.
current_use: Lesbian and feminist remembrance symbol; sometimes used in activism.

name: Labrys
description: A double-headed axe used in ancient Minoan culture, adopted by lesbian and feminist communities in the 1970s as a symbol of strength and independence. The Labrys lesbian pride flag (1999) features a black labrys on a violet background.
current_use: Lesbian and feminist empowerment symbol.

name: Caduceus / Rainbow Rod
description: A caduceus (medical staff) rendered in rainbow colors, used to represent LGBTQ+ healthcare and the fight against AIDS. Used by various LGBTQ+ health organizations.
current_use: LGBTQ+ healthcare and HIV/AIDS awareness contexts.

name: Handshake Symbol
description: Two female symbols interlinked in a handshake gesture, used in early lesbian feminist print culture to represent solidarity and partnership.
current_use: Historical symbol; rare in modern usage.

name: Rainbow Flag
description: Designed by Gilbert Baker and first flown at the San Francisco Gay Freedom Day Parade on June 25, 1978. The original 8-stripe version had pink and turquoise; the current 6-stripe version became standard in 1979 due to fabric availability.
current_use: The primary symbol of the LGBTQ+ community worldwide.

name: Progress Pride Flag
description: A redesign of the rainbow flag by Daniel Quasar, adding a chevron of white, pink, and light blue (transgender colors) plus black and brown stripes (representing BIPOC communities and those lost to AIDS). Updated in 2021 with an intersex circle.
current_use: Increasingly adopted at Pride events and by organizations wishing to be explicitly inclusive of trans and BIPOC communities.

name: ACT UP Silence = Death
description: A poster and slogan created by the Silence = Death Project and adopted by ACT UP. A pink triangle on a black background with the phrase 'SILENCE = DEATH' below it. Became the defining visual of AIDS activism.
current_use: AIDS activism and LGBTQ+ remembrance contexts.

name: Red Ribbon
description: Created by Visual AIDS in 1991 as a symbol of AIDS awareness and support for people living with HIV. The red ribbon became the first awareness ribbon and inspired many other colored awareness ribbons.
current_use: HIV/AIDS awareness; worn especially during World AIDS Day (December 1).

name: Intersex Circle
origin_context: Morgan Carpenter / Intersex Human Rights Australia
description: A yellow circle with a purple outline or border, representing intersex people. Designed by Morgan Carpenter in 2013 to be free of gendered symbolism. The unbroken circle represents wholeness and completeness.
current_use: Intersex pride and rights symbol; incorporated into the Progress Pride Flag (2021 update).

name: Asexual Spade
description: The spade (♠) playing card suit symbol has been informally adopted in parts of the asexual community as a lighthearted identity symbol. AVEN itself uses a triangular logo. The spade is used because 'ace' is slang for asexual.
current_use: Informal asexual community symbol, especially online.

name: Demisexual Triangle
description: A half-filled triangle, often in black, white, grey, and purple — the demisexual flag colors — used to represent demisexual identity. Emerged from AVEN forum culture.
current_use: Demisexual community symbol.

name: Unicorn
description: The unicorn has been an informal symbol of the LGBTQ+ community, particularly the queer and bisexual communities, since at least the 1980s. It represents magic, uniqueness, and the fantastical. The term 'unicorn' also has specific meanings within polyamorous communities.
current_use: Informal LGBTQ+ pride symbol; widely used in merchandise and popular culture.

name: Bear Paw
description: A bear paw print is the symbol of the Bear community — a subculture within the gay male community that celebrates larger, hairier body types and associated masculine aesthetics. Became widespread after the founding of Bear magazine in 1987.
current_use: Bear community identity symbol.

name: Sapphic Symbol
description: Various flower-based symbols (violets, lavender) have been used as sapphic/lesbian symbols since antiquity, rooted in the poetry of Sappho of Lesbos. Violets and lavender specifically reference Sappho's poems about exchanging garlands with female lovers.
current_use: Sapphic, lesbian, and WLW identity symbol; especially the violet emoji (💜) and lavender.

name: Leather Pride Flag
description: Designed by Tony DeBlase and introduced at the International Mr. Leather contest in Chicago in 1989. Black and blue stripes represent leather and denim; white stripe represents purity; red heart represents the heart of the leather community.
current_use: Leather, kink, and BDSM community symbol within LGBTQ+ spaces.
